origin: East Asia — wild parent species native to China, Korea, and Japan; modern garden plants are Hemerocallis hybrids
habitat: Moist meadows, grassy slopes, forest margins, and streamsides in temperate East Asia
blurb: The workhorse perennial of the summer border: a clump-forming plant that throws up branched scapes of big funnel-shaped flowers over a long season, each bloom lasting just a single day before the next opens. The garden daylilies are Hemerocallis hybrids selected in every color — the one photographed here a deep maroon. Tough, adaptable, and near-indestructible in alkaline clay, they ask almost nothing; their one fault is that deer happily nibble the buds and flowers.
sun: Full sun (6+ hours) for best bloom; tolerates part shade (2–6 hours)
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established — a deep weekly soak in summer keeps bloom heaviest
spread: Clump-forming; clumps expand slowly outward and are divided to multiply or rejuvenate
seasons: Strap-shaped leaves flush bright green in spring; branched scapes of funnel flowers from early summer into late summer (each bloom lasts one day, fresh buds open daily); foliage yellows and dies back to the ground after frost — no winter presence
wildlife: Flowers draw butterflies, hummingbirds, and bees for nectar; not a notable larval host
deer: Not deer-resistant — frequently browsed; deer relish the buds and flowers
toxic: Toxic to cats (all parts; causes kidney failure — keep away from cats); non-toxic to dogs and horses. Flowers, buds, and tubers are eaten by people in Asian cooking, but cause stomach upset in some and should be eaten only in moderation

care.hardiness: Reliably hardy in USDA zones 3–9, so daylilies are bulletproof across the entire Front Range (Boulder is zone 5b–6a) — among the most cold-hardy and trouble-free perennials you can plant here. They survive deep cold, drought, poor soil, and neglect, and need no winter protection.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant bare-root divisions or potted clumps in spring (April–May) once the ground is workable, or in early fall (September) so roots establish before hard freeze — both windows work on the Front Range. Set the crown about 1 in. below the soil surface; plant too deep and bloom suffers. Water in well and keep the root zone moist through the first season. In containers daylilies grow fine through summer in a large (≥5 gallon) pot, but their roots are far less cold-hardy than in the ground — overwinter a potted daylily in an unheated garage or sink the pot in soil, or it can freeze out in a hard Boulder winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6 hours of direct sun — gives the heaviest bloom and sturdiest scapes. Daylilies tolerate part shade (2–6 hours, especially light afternoon shade in our intense high-altitude sun), but flowering thins noticeably in too much shade. Dark-red and purple cultivars hold their color better with a little afternoon shade, which keeps the petals from scorching.[1][2]
care.soil: Extremely adaptable — daylilies grow in clay, loam, or sand and tolerate Boulder's heavy alkaline clay without complaint. They prefer a moist, well-drained soil rich in organic matter for best performance, but handle lean and dry ground once established. The one thing to avoid is standing water in winter, which can rot the crown. Work compost into heavy clay at planting to improve drainage and structure.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate and forgiving. Once established, daylilies are genuinely drought-tolerant and one of the better-adapted perennials for our semi-arid climate. For the fullest bloom and largest flowers, give a deep soak about once a week through the hot summer bloom season; established clumps survive on far less. Avoid keeping the soil constantly wet. Water at the base in the morning rather than overhead.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in. apart (up to 30 in. for the largest cultivars) so each clump has room to expand. Daylilies fill in to a solid mass over two or three seasons.[1]
care.propagation: Division is the standard and easiest way to propagate, and the way to keep a named hybrid true (seed does not come true to the parent). Lift the whole clump in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer/early fall after bloom, and pull or cut it apart into fans — sections of 2–3 fans each, with roots attached. Replant promptly at the original depth and water in. Divide every 3–5 years (or whenever bloom declines and the center of the clump thins) to maintain vigor. Trimming the foliage back to about 6 in. when dividing reduces transplant stress.[1][2]
care.bloom: Each individual flower opens for a single day and closes by evening (hence Hemerocallis, 'beauty for a day'), but a well-grown scape carries many buds that open in succession, and a clump blooms for several weeks. Peak bloom on the Front Range runs early to late summer (roughly June through August), depending on cultivar — choosing early, mid, and late varieties (and reblooming types like 'Stella de Oro') extends the show across the whole summer. Removing the spent flowers and developing seedpods (deadheading) keeps the plant tidy and pushes energy into more buds.[1][2]
care.feeding: Light feeders. A single application of a balanced fertilizer or a topdressing of compost in early spring as growth resumes is plenty. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which produces lush leaves at the expense of flowers. Established clumps in decent soil often need no feeding at all.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low maintenance. Deadhead spent blooms and snap off forming seedpods to keep the clump blooming and tidy. Remove yellowing leaves through the season. After frost kills the foliage, cut the dead leaves to the ground in late fall or early spring to clean up the clump and remove overwintering pests. Divide every few years when bloom thins (see Propagation).[1][2]
care.troubles: Daylilies have no serious problems and are one of the most pest-free perennials for the region. Watch for: daylily rust (orange pustules on leaf undersides — uncommon here, remove affected foliage); aphids and thrips, which can streak or distort buds (a strong water spray or insecticidal soap handles them); and crown/root rot in soggy, poorly drained ground (improve drainage). The real nuisance on the Front Range is deer and rabbits, which browse the buds and flowers — fence or repel where browsing pressure is high.[1][2]
care.companions: Pair daylilies with other tough, sun-loving perennials that share their low-water, clay-tolerant habit: ornamental grasses (little bluestem, blue oat grass) and Russian sage for airy contrast behind the mounded foliage; coneflowers, black-eyed Susan, yarrow, and salvia for a long-blooming summer border; and spring bulbs interplanted in front, since the daylily's rising foliage hides the bulbs' fading leaves. Their fountain of strap leaves also makes a good front-of-border edging or massed groundcover on a sunny slope.[1][2]

edible.card: Flower buds & petals, and tubers, cooked — Asian cuisine
edible.summary: Daylily flowers, unopened buds, and the small tuberous roots are a documented food in Asian (especially Chinese) cooking — but eat only in moderation, only the true daylily, and never give any part to a cat.[1][3][4]
edible.parts: The **flowers and unopened buds** of Hemerocallis are the best-known edible part: the buds (picked young, about 1.5–2 in. long, before they turn bitter) are stir-fried or added to soups, and the petals can be eaten fresh in salads or dried — dried daylily buds are the 'golden needles' (gum jum) of Chinese cooking. The young tuberous **roots** are also eaten cooked, with a mild, nutty, water-chestnut-like flavor. Sources note the flavor of the cooked flowers as something like asparagus or zucchini.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Cook the buds and tubers — sauté, stir-fry, or add to soups; petals can be used raw as a garnish or dried for later use. Pick buds young, before they become bitter. Introduce daylily to your diet in small amounts the first time, as some people experience digestive upset (it can act as a mild diuretic or laxative). Use only flowers from plants you know have not been treated with pesticides.[3][4]
edible.caution: Two serious cautions. First, **daylily (Hemerocallis) is highly toxic to cats** — all parts, including the flowers, leaves, and even water from a vase, can cause acute kidney (renal) failure and death in cats; there is no safe amount for a cat. Never let a cat ingest any part, and keep cut flowers away from them. (It is non-toxic to dogs and horses.) Second, **do not confuse the daylily with the true lilies (Lilium spp.)** — they are unrelated plants despite the shared name; true lily flowers and bulbs are NOT the traditional edible and Lilium is likewise deadly to cats. For people, even genuine daylily should be eaten only in moderation and cooked, as raw or excessive amounts cause stomach upset, nausea, or diarrhea in some individuals.[1][3][4]
